exercises.description: Order the sentences (B-G) below to make a story. {v} The first sentence of the story (A) is given for you as an example. {v} A-Nelson Rolihlahla Mandela  was a South African anti-apartheid revolutionary,political leader.

B He was the country's first black Head of State.

E During his staying at the university, he became involved in anti-colonial and African nationalist politics.
C He declined a second presidential term, and in 1999 was succeeded by Thabo Mbeki

exercises.heading: Education
exercises.description: Person A {v} With the new advances in technology I’ve found that cyber schooling my kids is actually extremely beneficial to both me and them. Not only am I able to spend more time with them, I can have a hand in their learning and focus on things that I find to be healthy for their education. Of course, having a formal education in education has given me an advantage, but I believe many adults have the same thoughts as I do. {v} Person B {v} I have found cyber schooling to be extremely challenging, not only for myself but also for my children. They really find it difficult to be in a setting in which they are not surrounded by their friends and have a teacher who is focusing on a specific topic. While I do like the family time, I am not sure that I will choose to continue this form of education, especially as my kids grow older I feel that they need to be in a social setting. {v} Person C {v} My kids have been telling me that they absolutely love being able to learn using online resources and connecting with people through a screen. I am very happy that this is something they like, but it is difficult for me to have them around all the time. Whenever there is a concept that they don’t completely understand, I have to study it myself so that I am able to explain it to them in a clear way. I feel like I’m back in school sometimes. {v} Person D {v} I am unsure about why the government even has cyber school as a viable option when educating young children. When it comes to teenagers or young adults, I could see it as an option, but young children need constant supervision and I am not comfortable leaving them alone with electronics, even if it is for learning. I prefer having them at school during the week and enjoying weekend family time.
exercises.subtitle: Four people respond to an online magazine article about Cyber Schooling. Read their comments and answer the questions.
exercises.label: Reading Part 4

questions.title: 1. Who thinks that cyber schooling allows them to have more control of their children's education?
2. Who thinks that their opinion is shared by others in the same situation?
3. Who thinks that kids need to learn while being surrounded by other children?
4. Who finds it to be tiring having children at home being cyber-schooled?
5. Who feels like they are reviewing things they have already learned in the past?
6. Who thinks that cyber school is not a good idea?
7. Who is qualified to teach their children at home?

exercises.description: Read the text below. Match the headings A-H to the paragraphs 19-25.
exercises.label: Reading Part 5

answers: B. The relationship between the housing market and the economy
H.Why Baby Boomers are buying less houses
questions.title: 19. The trends of buying homes have evolved significantly over time. There are many factors {v} influencing the decision of home buyers. These include designs, locations, features and size, {v} among other things. The housing market affects the economy in many ways, and is itself also {v} affected by the economy. It’s this mutual relationship, one feeding off the other, that creates {v} such interest in paying close attention to the ups and downs of the housing market. If it’s {v} doing well, the rest of the economy is likely to see similar growth.

answers: A. Different groups of home buyers
questions.title: 20. According to recent research, there are 4 categories of home buyers, namely, the Millennials {v} (people born after approximately 1985), Gen X’ers (born in the 60s and 70s), Baby Boomers {v} (born in the 50s) and Seniors (born before World War II). The categories are decided by the age {v} of the buyers. The preferences of the buyers across these four age groups differ {v} considerably. However, all buyers were somewhat interested in energy-efficient homes. Other {v} requirements generally consist of laundry rooms, garage storage, and walk-in pantries. Over {v} 80 percent of buyers chose these features. Some other features were hardwood floors, {v} patios, and external lighting.

answers: E. Most preferred features amongst potential house owners
questions.title: 21. Home buyers expressed their wishes for a number of features. 92% of homeowners expressed {v} that a laundry room was one of the most desirable features. 90% wanted Energy Star {v} appliances and external lighting. More than 85 percent of potential homeowners spoke about {v} their preference for Energy Star rating all over the home, Energy Star windows and ceiling fans. {v} 84% of people considered a patio to be an important feature. 83% of people wanted a full {v} bath on the first floor of their house. Around 80% of potential homeowners desired features like, {v} garage storage, kitchen table space and walk-in pantries. These figures indicate what the {v} most desired features are for homeowners.

answers: F. Most-wanted attributes as per generation
questions.title: 22. All 4 generations of potential homeowners expressed their most wanted features, but there were {v} differences in how much these groups preferred each feature. For instance, people from the {v} Millennial, Gen X, and Senior categories had laundry rooms on top of their priority list. On the {v} other hand, people from the Baby Boomer age group voted for Energy Star rated appliances {v} as their most desired feature. For Millennials, the next most preferred features included {v} exterior lighting, living room, patio, and front porch. Gen X people liked Energy Star rated {v} appliances, exterior lighting, and Energy Star rating for the whole home. Energy Star ratings for {v} home and appliances were some of the top priorities of Baby Boomers and {v} Seniors.

answers: C. Undesirable features amongst potential house owners
questions.title: 23. Some features are not considered essential or desirable in homes. The results of the study {v} aggregated responses to determine the most unwanted features. The top most unwanted {v} character in new homes was an elevator. 63% of those polled said that they did not need an {v} elevator. The next most unwanted feature was a pet washing station, as 54% of people {v} responded against it. 53% of home owners expressed that golf courses were unnecessary. This {v} was followed by a day care centre, which 52% of people said was not needed. About 46% of {v} those polled commented on their dislike for high-density living spaces. More than 40% voted {v} for cork flooring and master bathrooms with dual toilets as the next most unwanted features.

answers: G. Undesirable features according to generation
questions.title: 24. There were some differences between which features of homes were the most unwanted, {v} depending on age groups of people who voted. Elevators were the most common unwanted {v} feature by people from the Millennial, Gen X, and Baby Boomers age groups. Seniors {v} considered a daycare centre as the least wanted feature. Millennials and Gen X’ers considered {v} golf courses as the next most unwanted features. The second most unwanted features of {v} Baby Boomers and Seniors were daycare centre and pet washing station, respectively. The 3rd {v} and 4th most unwanted features for Millennials were high-density communities and pet {v} washing stations. Pet washing stations and wine cellars were unpopular with all the groups.

answers: D. The Millennial effect on the housing market
questions.title: 25. Millennials, in general, have been seen as a threat to the housing market. Because of inflation, {v} absurd amounts of student debt, difficulty to find work, and unlivable wages, over 30% of {v} Millennials in the U.S. still live at their parents’ house. This is a significant number when {v} compared with this same age range, 18-34, in the 1960s when only 20% still lived with their {v} parents. This inability to move out on their own has taken a serious hit on the real estate {v} industry, and economists are worried about what this might mean for the future. Recent years {v} have begun to see some improvement, but there’s still a way to go.

questions.title: Question 1: What is her opinion on the way women are viewed in the workplace? {x}
answers: She thinks that women have to work harder than men to show their value.
She thinks that women and men have achieved the same amount of success.
She thinks that gender equality in the workplace is being given the proper amount of attention.

questions.title: Question 2: What is her opinion on the changes that must be made? {x}
answers: She thinks that men need to contribute to domestic tasks so it is not the woman's responsibility.
She thinks that it is okay for men to be paid more than women as long as they put more effort into their work
She thinks that it is a good idea for children to decide who they want to take care of them at home.

questions.title: Question 1: What does the man think about obesity? {x}
answers: It is a serious problem that is caused by overeating and a sedentary lifestyle.
It is extremely difficult to live a fulfilling life while struggling with obesity.
It can result in medical problems that may last a lifetime.

questions.title: Question 2: What does the man think are common misunderstandings about obesity? {x}
answers: For those who are obese, it is a lifelong problem that cannot be turned around.
Obesity is not actually considered to be a clinical condition that must be treated.
The Body Mass Index is a precise way to know if someone is obese or not.

title: On Air
description: Step behind the mic — tell stories, describe your world, and connect with others through the power of your voice.
